title: Account Lifecycle
description: Learn the account lifecycle stages and how accounts move between Onboarding, Active, Dormant, and Blocked status.

sections.title: Account Lifecycle: The Customer Journey
sections.content:
Every customer relationship follows a predictable journey. Once a deal closes, the account enters the lifecycle management phase—a series of stages tracked at the Parent Account level that tell you the real commercial status of the customer relationship.

Understanding these stages is critical. It's how you know which customers generate revenue, which are at risk, and which represent growth opportunities.

sections.title: Onboarding: Getting Customers Live
sections.content:
The moment a deal closes, the account enters Onboarding—a transition stage where the customer has signed but isn't yet generating value. This phase typically lasts 2-4 months and involves:

- System configuration and environment setup
- Customer training and enablement
- Integration with existing tools and workflows
- Pre-launch testing and issue resolution
- Executive alignment and go-live readiness validation

The goal is to move the customer from signed contract to live usage. If onboarding stretches beyond 90 days, escalate it. The first 90 days determine long-term success. Customers who go live smoothly and quickly see faster time-to-value, experience lower churn, and become better expansion candidates.

This stage is owned by the customer success team, with support from Sales (relationship continuity), Implementation (technical setup), and Finance (billing verification).

Transition to Active: Active status is triggered when the customer's systems go live and usage confirms they're consuming services as expected—verified by the success team, not just date-based.

sections.title: Active: The Revenue-Generating State
sections.content:
Active is the goal state. This is where accounts generate revenue, deliver customer value, and create opportunities for expansion and renewal.

Characteristics of Active Accounts:
- Current, paid contracts with clear start and end dates
- On-time invoice payments (no billing disputes)
- Consistent product usage aligned with contract expectations
- Regular engagement with your team through check-ins and reviews
- Net Promoter Score tracking and satisfaction monitoring
- Documented expansion opportunities and renewal pipeline

Account Manager Responsibilities:
- Quarterly business reviews demonstrating value and ROI
- Proactive identification of expansion opportunities (new users, additional products, higher tiers)
- Renewal cycle management starting 6+ months before contract end
- Executive relationship building at the customer's leadership level
- Risk mitigation if satisfaction dips or usage declines

What Success Looks Like:
A healthy Active account shows stable or growing usage, expanding per-user adoption, timely contract renewals (6+ months before expiration), and positive feedback from customer stakeholders. These accounts become case studies, referral sources, and your most profitable business.

Transition Risk: Active accounts can slip to Dormant if renewal negotiations stall, to Blocked if billing issues arise, or if usage collapses and the customer deprioritizes your solution.

sections.title: Dormant: Former Customers, But Not Abandoned
sections.content:
Dormant accounts are former customers—the contract expired without renewal or the customer explicitly discontinued. This is not failure; it's a natural part of the business cycle.

Why Accounts Become Dormant:
- Contract expiration without renewal (price sensitivity, budget cuts, product misfit)
- Explicit customer request to discontinue
- Company merger, acquisition, or organizational restructuring
- Replacement with a competitor due to feature gaps or better fit
- Change in customer leadership or priorities eliminating the use case

Dormant Management Strategy:
Dormant accounts are monitored and maintained for reactivation potential. Circumstances change—new leadership, fresh budget cycles, problem statements resurfacing, or competitive displacement creating opportunity.

Account Manager Actions:
- Quarterly outreach (email, call, or business review invitation)
- Tracking reason for discontinuation and monitoring for changes
- Building business case for win-back (ROI, new features, market changes)
- Staying on customer mailing lists for product announcements
- Responding immediately if customer signals interest in re-engagement

Win-Back Economics:
Reactivating a Dormant customer costs significantly less than acquiring a new one. They understand your product, have trained staff, and have existing integrations. A dormant customer who returns often becomes a higher-value account than they were initially.

Even low-touch dormant management creates competitive advantage. When a customer's competitor uses you, they want you. When a dormant customer faces a new challenge your solution solves, you're already known and trusted.

sections.title: Blocked: When Issues Demand Immediate Action
sections.content:
Blocked accounts have critical issues preventing normal operations. This is a critical flag requiring immediate escalation and resolution.

Common Blocking Reasons:
- Unpaid invoices or billing disputes
- Legal or compliance issues (data protection breach, contract violation)
- Contract violations (non-payment, security breach)
- Operational incidents requiring investigation

What Happens When Blocked:
- New orders and renewals are frozen until resolution
- Service delivery may be paused or restricted
- Finance escalates payment collection
- Legal or Compliance teams engage to manage risk
- Senior leadership becomes involved in customer communication
- Regular, transparent communication works toward resolution
- Account does NOT count as Active in revenue recognition or health metrics

Resolution Path:
Blocked status is temporary. Resolution requires:
- Root cause identification and communication to customer
- Clear action plan and timeline for fixing the issue
- Executive sponsorship ensuring accountability
- Customer sign-off confirming issue resolution
- Transition back to Active (if relationship continues) or Dormant (if customer churned during block)

Preventative Discipline:
Most Blocked situations are preventable through proactive account management. Quarterly reviews catch billing issues before they escalate. Regular communication prevents legal surprises. Usage monitoring alerts you to customer dissatisfaction before they go silent.

Financial Impact:
Blocked accounts represent at-risk revenue. Extended Blocks can result in contract termination, payment loss, or legal costs. Resolving Blocks quickly protects both revenue and brand reputation.

sections.title: Stage Transitions: Event-Driven, Not Time-Based
sections.content:
Accounts don't move between stages automatically by calendar. They transition when business events occur.

Onboarding → Active: Customer system goes live and begins consuming services. Success team confirms go-live, validates usage metrics, and documents customer readiness.

Active → Dormant: Contract expires without renewal or customer discontinues service. Account manager updates status within 5 business days.

Active → Blocked: Billing dispute, legal issue, compliance violation, or service incident prevents normal operations. Immediate notification to Finance, Legal, and management.

Blocked → Active: Issue resolved, validated by responsible party, and customer relationship normalizes. Requires documentation and customer sign-off.

Dormant → Active: Customer signs new contract or requests reactivation. Sales and Success coordinate transition plan and restart engagement cadence.

Automation:
Lifecycle status changes should trigger automated workflows: renewal reminders (90 days before contract end), success planning (onboarding launch), check-in tasks (quarterly Active reviews), and escalation alerts (Blocked issues). These prevent human error and ensure consistent follow-up.

sections.title: Why Lifecycle Status Matters
sections.content:
Lifecycle status is not administrative data—it powers core business functions. Accuracy directly impacts company decisions and financial reporting.

Revenue Recognition: Finance uses Active account counts to recognize contract revenue. GAAP and ASC 606 require accurate documentation of customer status.

Forecasting and Pipeline Health: Leadership relies on Active counts and churn rates to assess business health. Accurate lifecycle status enables predictive churn modeling, realistic expansion pipeline analysis, and board-ready business health assessments.

Resource Allocation: Active accounts receive ongoing account management and expansion focus. Onboarding accounts receive intensive success support. Dormant accounts receive low-touch win-back outreach. Blocked accounts receive specialized support. Wrong status means wrong resource allocation.

Customer Experience: Customers in different lifecycle stages expect different engagement. Active customers expect quarterly business reviews. Dormant customers expect occasional check-ins. Blocked customers expect crisis management.

The Data Quality Foundation: Lifecycle status is the summary metric that validates all other account data. If lifecycle is accurate, account structure is probably correct.

Keep lifecycle status current. Make it a monthly audit activity. It's the foundation of accurate data and smart resource allocation.

keyTakeaways: Accounts progress through Onboarding → Active → Dormant or Blocked based on customer status and business events
Accurate account lifecycle status is essential for revenue recognition, forecasting, and resource allocation
Blocked status requires escalation and resolution before normal business can resume
